architecture_summary: This paper is a survey that systematically reviews Transformer schemes for time series modeling from two perspectives: network modifications and application domains. It also presents empirical analyses on robustness, model size, and seasonal-trend decomposition for time series Transformers.

novel_contributions: A comprehensive and systematic survey of Transformers for time series modeling.
A new taxonomy of time series Transformers from the perspectives of network modifications and application domains.
Extensive empirical studies on robustness analysis, model size analysis, and seasonal-trend decomposition analysis.
A discussion of future research directions for time series Transformers.

key_claims.claim: This is the first work to comprehensively and systematically review the key developments of Transformers for modeling time series data.
The paper proposes a new taxonomy for time series Transformers based on network modifications and application domains.
The survey categorizes time series Transformers into forecasting, anomaly detection, and classification, and further discusses network-level and architecture-level modifications.
key_claims.evidence_section: 3 Taxonomy of Transformers in Time Series

key_claims.claim: The paper conducts empirical studies on robustness, model size, and seasonal-trend decomposition to examine how Transformers perform in time series.
The robustness experiment shows that various Transformer-based models deteriorate quickly when the input sequence length is prolonged.
The model size experiment suggests that Transformers with 3 to 6 layers often achieve better results than deeper variants on ETTm2.
The seasonal-trend decomposition analysis shows that a simple decomposition block can significantly boost performance by 50 % to 80%.
